refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total operational time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the availability target, specifies bidirectionality (command uplink + telemetry downlink), and anchors measurement to an agreed evaluation window. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to loss-of-control incidents.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) during field trials; log timestamps of outages; calculate availability = (total_time - sum_outage_durations) / total_time; compare against 0.999 threshold and customer-defined acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link degradation or loss and report status to safety services.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall support link failover or recovery mechanisms to minimize outage duration.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant wireless communication paths (primary + backup cellular/radio) with automatic failover logic and continuous link health monitoring.
DP-B: Deploy a single high-reliability wireless link with error correction and retransmission protocols, backed by operator manual recovery procedures.
DP-C: Implement a hybrid wired-wireless architecture where vehicle operates tethered for critical tasks and wireless for non-critical tasks, with mode switching logic.
DP-D: Establish a network abstraction layer that aggregates multiple communication bearers (cellular, satellite, mesh radio) with intelligent routing and failover orchestration.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Implement redundant wireless communication paths (primary + backup cellular/radio) with automatic failover logic and continuous link health monitoring.

potential_sources: Redundancy logic couples to vehicle power budget (backup link consumes additional power).
Failover timing couples to safety services alert latency (must notify Safety Services within acceptable window).
Link health monitoring couples to telemetry bandwidth (status messages consume uplink capacity).
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are manageable via interface contracts (Telemetry, SafetyAlert). DP-A is decoupled from specific wireless technology choice, allowing flexibility in bearer selection. Recommended to defer technology selection (4G/5G vs. proprietary radio) to next decomposition level.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Dual wireless bearers can be provisioned at the worksite (cellular coverage + backup radio infrastructure available).
Assumption: Automatic failover logic can be implemented with <100 ms switchover latency to meet safety requirements.

Risk: Correlated failure of both bearers (e.g., site-wide RF jamming, simultaneous network outages) could still cause total link loss; mitigation requires operational procedures and safety interlocks.
Risk: Redundancy adds cost and complexity; customer may not accept if link availability target is relaxed.
Risk: Backup bearer may have higher latency or lower bandwidth, affecting tele-operator experience and safety responsiveness.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify with Safety Manager and Customer: (1) Is 0.999 availability a hard requirement or negotiable? (2) What is the acceptable failover latency? (3) Are there site-specific constraints on backup bearer availability? (4) Should DP-A include a tertiary bearer (e.g., satellite) for extreme scenarios? Recommend proceeding with DP-A but deferring bearer technology selection and failover timing to next level.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end bidirectional communication path between tele-operator and vehicle, including link establishment, health monitoring, failover orchestration, and status reporting. It abstracts the underlying wireless bearers and provides a unified interface to Vehicle (for command reception and telemetry transmission) and Safety Services (for link status alerts).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from tele-operator console; Teleoperation module decodes and forwards to Vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, sensor readings, link quality metrics) from Vehicle to tele-operator console; Teleoperation module aggregates and formats.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Reports link status events (loss, degradation, recovery) to Safety Services for operator notification and emergency procedures.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts underlying wireless bearers (primary and backup); Teleoperation module manages bearer selection, failover, and health checks.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic, failover orchestration, and status reporting. All four sub-FRs (command uplink, telemetry downlink, link detection, failover) map cleanly to this module. Minimal diffusion: only Network Link bearer management is external (owned by infrastructure/network team).

Poor containment: Vehicle owns the physical radio hardware and antenna, but link availability logic (failover, health monitoring, redundancy orchestration) should not reside in Vehicle firmware. High diffusion risk: scattering failover logic across Vehicle and Teleoperation would create coupling and maintenance burden.

Poor containment: Safety Services owns the response to link loss (e.g., emergency stop, operator alert), but not the detection or failover logic. High diffusion: mixing link management with safety response logic violates separation of concerns.
Poor containment: NGHE Solution is the top-level system; assigning DP-A here would diffuse implementation across multiple sub-modules. Not appropriate for detailed design ownership.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-A (redundant wireless paths with failover). It has clear ownership of the communication abstraction layer, interfaces cleanly with Vehicle (via Tasking/Telemetry) and Safety Services (via SafetyAlert), and can manage Network Link bearer selection without diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle firmware is tightly coupled to a single wireless bearer, Teleoperation may struggle to implement transparent failover without Vehicle-side changes. Mitigation: Ensure Vehicle exposes a bearer-agnostic command/telemetry interface.

Risk: Network Link interface may not fully abstract bearer-level details (e.g., latency, packet loss rates). Mitigation: Define Network Link contract to include link quality metrics (RSSI, latency, error rate) so Teleoperation can make intelligent failover decisions.
Risk: Failover logic timing may interact with Safety Services response latency. Mitigation: Establish clear SLA for SafetyAlert notification (e.g., <50 ms after link loss detected).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm with Vehicle and Network teams: (1) Does Vehicle expose a bearer-agnostic interface for command/telemetry? (2) Does Network Link provide real-time link quality metrics? (3) What is the acceptable latency for SafetyAlert notification? Proceed with Teleoperation as owner, but document interface contracts and failover timing requirements.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (redundant wireless paths with failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (link availability ≥ 0.999). Redundancy and automatic failover are the primary mechanisms to achieve high availability. DP-A is technology-agnostic and allows flexibility in bearer selection. No decoupling or independence violations detected.

diffusion_risks: Failover logic must not be scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Mitigation: Teleoperation owns orchestration; Vehicle and Safety Services are consumers of status.
Network Link bearer management must be clearly separated from Teleoperation failover logic. Mitigation: Define Network Link interface contract to abstract bearer details.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score. Minimal diffusion (0.05) limited to Network Link bearer abstraction, which is appropriate. No high-risk diffusion detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High: Link availability can be measured directly via continuous monitoring of link state transitions. Failover events and outage durations are loggable and auditable. Teleoperation module can expose metrics (uptime, failover count, mean time to recovery) for verification.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear: Teleoperation owns link availability delivery. Vehicle provides command/telemetry transport. Safety Services consumes link status alerts. Interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined.

recommended_revisions: Refine Network Link interface contract to specify link quality metrics (RSSI, latency, packet loss) and failover trigger thresholds.
Define SafetyAlert SLA: maximum latency from link loss detection to Safety Services notification (recommend <50 ms).
Establish acceptance criteria for failover latency: maximum time from primary bearer loss to backup bearer operational (recommend <100 ms for safety-critical operations).
Document assumptions about worksite bearer availability (cellular coverage, backup radio infrastructure) and obtain customer confirmation.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (command uplink, telemetry downlink, link detection, failover recovery). DP-A is sound and well-contained in Teleoperation module, but further refinement is needed to address sub-FR details, bearer technology selection, failover timing, and interface contracts. Proceeding to next decomposition level is justified and necessary.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle with <X ms latency and <Y% packet loss.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator with <X ms latency and <Y% packet loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link degradation (latency >X ms, packet loss >Y%, or bearer unavailable) within <Z ms and report status to Safety Services.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall automatically failover from primary to backup wireless bearer within <W ms and resume command/telemetry flow without operator intervention.

assumptions: Dual wireless bearers (primary + backup) can be provisioned at the worksite.
Automatic failover logic can achieve <100 ms switchover latency.
Vehicle exposes a bearer-agnostic command/telemetry interface.
Network Link interface provides real-time link quality metrics (RSSI, latency, error rate).
Safety Services can respond to SafetyAlert notifications within acceptable latency (<50 ms).
Customer-defined evaluation period is ≥30 days to ensure statistical significance of availability measurement.
open_risks: Correlated failure of both wireless bearers (RF jamming, simultaneous network outages) could still cause total link loss; requires operational procedures and safety interlocks.
Backup bearer may have higher latency or lower bandwidth, affecting tele-operator experience and safety responsiveness.
Failover timing may interact with Safety Services response latency; requires careful SLA definition.
Worksite may not have backup bearer infrastructure available; customer acceptance depends on feasibility assessment.
evidence_needed: Confirmation from Customer and Safety Manager: Is 0.999 availability a hard requirement or negotiable?
Confirmation from Vehicle team: Does Vehicle expose bearer-agnostic interface?
Confirmation from Network team: Can Network Link provide link quality metrics?
Worksite assessment: What wireless bearers are available (cellular, radio, satellite)?
Failover timing analysis: What is the minimum achievable switchover latency given hardware and software constraints?
Safety analysis: What are the consequences of link loss? What are the acceptable failover latencies?
